review_comment:
This is actually now strange, because this function either returns `length`, which is `size_t` or result of `sizeof()` assigned to `length`, which means it returns `size_t`.
It does not seem that it can fail so it should probably just return `size_t`.

This function could fail if a parameter were invalid.

But then again, it should probably also fail if the device ID exceeds `INT32_MAX` in size 😅.

It's probably safe.

I hate `size_t`, it is pointless type. `sizeof` should be returning `unsigned int`.
OK, I guess they have figure out that the `int` is size of device int register and memory objects may be bigger than that, but how have they failed to see that half of C code uses inbound error reporting and did not define `ssize_t` with it?

The C standard only states that `size_t` should have conversion rank not greater than `signed long` (+ the implementation specific magic words), so it does not seem that there is easy way to find out what is best arithmetic type equivalent. So `int` it is.

`size_t` is standard C (not POSIX), and is used pretty ubiquitously this way. It's not really a concern in this PR which is just addressing the layering violation. If `int` is otherwise fine, should this comment be marked as resolved?

> `size_t` is standard C (not POSIX), and is used pretty ubiquitously this way. It's not really a concern in this PR which is just addressing the layering violation. If `int` is otherwise fine, should this comment be marked as resolved?

Yes, I know. It just shows how the language standard is out of touch with reality.
We go with int and cry.
Is this really correct? This driver is for native_sim, and some 10 lines later the return value from the host libc's `read()` is assigned to it.
The definitions should be identical (as far as the underlying type goes). Otherwise, there would be warnings / errors.

I don't doubt that. I was mainly wondering where a `native_sim` driver like this conceptually sits in the software stack. It's calling the host OS APIs (like `read()`) so in that sense it could be seen to be above POSIX (in which case `ssize_t` would be more appropriate), however the driver also accesses Zephyr kernel APIs. Anyway, this isn't necessarily that critical (as you say, the types should be the same), which is why I didn't want to block this PR by requesting changes, but I do think it would be good to understand where `native_sim` driver code sits in the software stack.

Yea, it's kind of a recursion. I would still say those drivers sit below (Zephyr's) POSIX API.

The namespace issue is kind of an unfortunate side-effect (not without solutions, ofc)
